text: The surgeon employs a lineal approach to excise an ischiorectal lipoma, focusing on the precise removal of the fatty tumor from the ischiorectal fossa.
A thirty-seven years old woman with no medical history and a soft mass in the gluteal region was assisted.
A CT scan was performed and showed an ischiorectal fossa mass with fifteen centimeters approximately. without infiltration sinks or intratumoral vascularization.
We choose a perineal minimally invasive approach because of the age of the patient and the localization of the tumor in minor pelvis.
The patient is positioned in the jackknife position on the operating table, with the main surgeon and assistant surgeon standing on opposite sides of the patient.
The surgeon makes a semicircular incision in the anodermal skin, preparing to access the underlying structures.
The surgeon places a long star retractor to enhance the exposure of the surgical site, providing a clear view of the area for further intervention.
The surgeon performs dissection of the subcutaneous tissue using electrocautery, revealing the external sphincter muscle beneath the anodermal skin.
The surgeon carefully dissects the mass using bipolar energy with ligature, desiccating the inner wall of the ischiorectal fossa while preserving the sphincter apparatus.

The surgeon performs a careful digital dissection to separate the tumor from the obturator fascia and obturator internus, minimizing bleeding. Once fully separated, the mass is eviscerated from the surgical site.
The final size of the tumor was fourteen point eight plus seven point two centimeters. Definitive pathology exam was a giant lipoma without infiltration of resection margins.
The surgeon removes the long star retractor and sutures the ischiorectal fossa with single stitches using 2-0 Vicryl suture material.
The surgeon approximates the skin edges using a continuous intradermal suture technique with a 3-0 monofilament.
The patient advised were to maintenance the skin clean and dry.
She was discharged at two days postoperative without one infection or surgical site abscess. In the outpatient revision, the patient was asymptomatic without signs of anal incontinence.
